text.title: Predictive Analytics in PM — Seven Antipatterns
text.summary: The seven most common failure modes when applying ML-based forecasting to project management, from garbage-in data quality to per-person productivity abuse.

antipattern.description: Garbage-in status fields — "In Progress" means "abandoned" or "waiting for review" depending on the team member.
antipattern.reason: Every metric computed from inconsistent status fields lies. A model trained on this data will forecast confidently based on noise. Data quality discipline is a prerequisite to any forecasting investment.

antipattern.description: Concept drift ignored — model trained on Q1 team composition and process used in Q4 after three re-orgs and a process overhaul.
antipattern.reason: ML models learn the statistical regularities of a specific team and process. When the team or process changes significantly, the model's learned patterns are no longer valid. Silent drift produces increasingly wrong but equally confident forecasts.

antipattern.description: Survivorship bias — training data includes only completed projects, excluding cancelled and silent-fail projects.
antipattern.reason: Cancelled projects carry the failure patterns the model most needs to learn. A dataset of completions only under-predicts cancellation risk and over-predicts completion probability.

antipattern.description: Uncalibrated probabilities — model emits "70% likely to slip" without a calibration dataset to validate that claim.
antipattern.reason: An uncalibrated probability is an opinion dressed as a number. PM teams that make budget or scope decisions on uncalibrated confidence intervals will be systematically misled.

antipattern.description: Causal claims from correlational features — "long descriptions correlate with overrun" → PM bans long descriptions.
antipattern.reason: Correlation in issue data is not causal. Intervening on a correlate without understanding the mechanism often removes a useful signal without changing the underlying cause.

antipattern.description: Per-person productivity metrics emitted from issue data (velocity per developer, bug rate per engineer).
antipattern.reason: Individual-level metrics from trackers are an HR and legal risk, incentivize gaming (artificially inflating closed tickets), and destroy the team-level signal that makes forecasting valuable.

antipattern.description: Stale snapshot forecast — forecast generated yesterday from data a week old, presented as current to stakeholders.
antipattern.reason: PM data ages fast; a week-old snapshot misses the sprint that just closed, the risk that materialized, or the scope change that was approved. Every forecast must show a data-freshness timestamp.
